0: <title>Using a life cycle assessment method to determine the environmental impacts of manure utilisation: biogas plant and composting systems </title>
T. HishinumaA H. KurishimaB, C. YangA and Y. Genchi^

^National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology, Research Center for Life Cycle Assessment, 16-1 Onogawa, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-8569, Japan. BShibaura Institute of Technology, 307 Fukasaku, Minumaku, Saitama, Saitama 337-8570, Japan. Corresponding author. Email: t-hishinuma@aist.go.jp

Abstract. The aim of this study was to use life cycle assessment methods to determine the environmental impacts of manure utilisation by a biogas plant and by a typical manure composting system. The functional unit was defined as the average annual manure utilisation on a dairy farm with 100 cows. The environmental impact categories chosen were emissions of greenhouse gases (GHG) and acidification gases (AG). The GHG emissions were estimated as: 345.9t CO2-equivalents (e) for solid composting (case 1), 625.4t CO2-e for solid and liquid composting (case 2), and 86.3-90.1 1 CO2-e for the biogas plant system. The AG emissions were estimated as: 10.1 SO2-e for case 1, 18.4t SO2-e for case 2, and 13.1-24.2t SO2-e for the biogas plant system. These results show that a biogas plant system produces low GHG emissions, but comparatively high AG emissions with land application. It is suggested that land application using band spread or shallow injection attachments will decrease AG emissions (NH3) from biogas plant systems.

Abstract. The aim of this study was to use life cycle assessment methods to determine the environmental impacts of manure utilisation by a biogas plant and by a typical manure composting system. 2: Gaseous emissions from manure utilisation are considered to be one of the major sources of greenhouse gases (GHG) and acidification gases (AG) in Japan. GHG emissions from manure treatment and application contributed 26.3% of agricultural GHG emissions [27.41 CO2-equivalents (e)] in Japan in 2005 (CGER 2007). Manure treatment and subsequent land application also contributed to emissions of ammonia (NH3), causing acidification and odour problems for surrounding areas (Hojito et al. 2006). In Japan the typical process of manure treatment is composting or slurry aeration in open systems. These open systems are considered to be the main source of gas emissions.

Recently, a biogas plant with anaerobic digestion was constructed for cow manure treatment in Hokkaido, Japan. It was considered to be a low-emissions plant (RGUEC 2006) because of its closed configuration. However, the quantitative evaluation of environmental impacts at a biogas plant has not been assessed for total systems incorporating manure utilisation. The purpose of this study was to assess the environmental impacts of manure utilisation from different systems - composting and biogas plant - and to use the life cycle assessment (LCA) method to consider the environmental impact of a biogas plant.
3: <header>Materials and methods </header>

LCA is a method for assessing and comparing the environmental aspects and potential impacts of products or systems. Its basic procedures have been standardised in the ISO14040 series. LCA has four phases: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) inventory analysis; (iii) impact assessment; and (iv) interpretation.
The aim of the present study was to assess environmental impacts on manure utilisation systems for 100 cows. The three manure utilisation systems assessed were:

<list>(1) case 1 - composting at a compost depot and then application by 5-m3 manure spreader (MS); 
(2) case - solid and liquid composting with compost depot and slurry store and then application by MS and 5000-L slurry spreader with splash plate (SP: spreading slurry from squirt plate to the air); and 
(3) case biogas plant for slurry treatment and then application by a slurry tanker (with three attachments and capacity of 14 000 L).</list>

The land application of digested slurry used some application technologies (attachments for dispersal) to reduce NH3 emission during spreading. We compared efficiency of NH3 reduction and fuel consumption of these attachments: SP, band spread (BS, spilling to surface through a trailing hoses) and shallow injection (SI, injecting slurry into soil with open slot, 3 cm depth).

The system boundary of this study included processes of manure treatment and application (Fig. 1). Transportation processes were omitted from the analysis because of the high variability of transport distances. The life cycle stages considered were: (i) construction; (ii) production and utilisation of manure processing equipment; and (iii) upstream resources. The functional unit was defined as the annual manure utilisation (manure treatment and application) at a dairy farm with 100 cows.

The assessed environmental burdens were gaseous emissions of CO2, methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NOx), sulfur oxides (SOx) and NH3, which are the main environmental consequences of manure utilisation. The

environmental impacts were defined as global warming potential (GWP) and deposition-oriented acidification potential (DAP) for impact assessment. It calculated GHG and AG emission for evaluation of GWP and DAP.

The process model components (manure treatment and application processes) of each case were determined by material usage and by-product data from the literature e.g. such as capacity of equipments, fuel and electric power consumption (LEIO 1998; RGUEC 2006). It also used data determined by Ministry of Environment in Japan for the CH4 and N2O emissions coefficient at manure treatment and application processes (MOE 2002). The NH3 emission coefficient of manure treatment and application processes were applied to the data from previous research on composting and spreading (Maeda et al. 2001; Yokohama and Nakagawa 2005). The emissions data from production and combustion of fossil fuels, or electricity which was consumed during manure treatment and application, were applied from the LCA software JEMAI-LCApro (Japan Environmental Management Association for Industry). The data of CO2, NOx and SOX emissions from construction of equipment and machinery were used from the inventory of 3EID (Nansai et al. 2002).

The process model data and emissions coefficient data were collected from system component and environmental emissions at experience data, applying example and authorised inventory data. Consequently, the process model used characteristics of
manure utilisation systems to assess the environmental impacts of the treatments.

8: <header>Materials and methods </header>

LCA is a method for assessing and comparing the environmental aspects and potential impacts of products or systems. Its basic procedures have been standardised in the ISO14040 series. LCA has four phases: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) inventory analysis; (iii) impact assessment; and (iv) interpretation.
The aim of the present study was to assess environmental impacts on manure utilisation systems for 100 cows. The three manure utilisation systems assessed were:

<list>(1) case 1 - composting at a compost depot and then application by 5-m3 manure spreader (MS); 
(2) case - solid and liquid composting with compost depot and slurry store and then application by MS and 5000-L slurry spreader with splash plate (SP: spreading slurry from squirt plate to the air); and 
(3) case biogas plant for slurry treatment and then application by a slurry tanker (with three attachments and capacity of 14 000 L).</list>

The land application of digested slurry used some application technologies (attachments for dispersal) to reduce NH3 emission during spreading. We compared efficiency of NH3 reduction and fuel consumption of these attachments: SP, band spread (BS, spilling to surface through a trailing hoses) and shallow injection (SI, injecting slurry into soil with open slot, 3 cm depth).

The system boundary of this study included processes of manure treatment and application (Fig. 1). Transportation processes were omitted from the analysis because of the high variability of transport distances. The life cycle stages considered were: (i) construction; (ii) production and utilisation of manure processing equipment; and (iii) upstream resources. The functional unit was defined as the annual manure utilisation (manure treatment and application) at a dairy farm with 100 cows.

The assessed environmental burdens were gaseous emissions of CO2, methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NOx), sulfur oxides (SOx) and NH3, which are the main environmental consequences of manure utilisation. The

9: Gaseous emissions were calculated from each process in cases 1-3. Table 1 shows the accompanying life cycle inventory of manure utilisation systems. The biogas plant had a co- generator that consumes biogas (CH4) and, therefore, required no utility electricity or fuel. Thus, emissions from energy resources consumption for case 3 were set to zero. Digested slurry application with SI resulted in the lowest NH3 emissions but required more fuel than application with SP or BS. The difference in fuel consumption during application was caused by differences in operational performance (width of spreading) and traction load from injector depth.

The estimated GHG emissions were 345.9 CO2-e for case 1, 625.4t CO2-e for case 2 and 86.3-90.1 CO2-e for case 3 (Fig. 2). In general, the utilisation stage (especially treatment processes) had the largest GHG impact for case 1 and case 2, primarily caused by N2O emissions during composting. Total GHG emissions were lowest for case 3, with only 15-25% for case 1 and case 2. This was due to the restriction of gaseous emissions during manure treatment at the biogas plant, i.e. an anaerobic digestion system (closed system). 16: A biogas plant was found to have a lower GWP impact when compared with the composting systems using LCA because it uses a closed system for manure digestions. The life cycle acidification impact of a biogas plant was also comparable to other composting systems. Results indicate a trade-off among NH3 producing processes used in the biogas plant and the composting systems. For future manure processing, it is recommended that digested slurry is applied with SI and that slurry stores are equipped with sealed covering to prevent
gas emissions. These improvements will reduce AG emissions, especially the emission of NH3, as well as GHG emissions, thereby decreasing the environmental impacts of these systems.
